headline: Venus Is Hotter Than Mercury
body: Mercury is closer to the Sun, but Venus is the hottest planet of all. Its thick air is full of carbon dioxide, which traps heat like a blanket. The surface can pass 870°F — hot enough to melt lead. This runaway “greenhouse effect” turned Venus into a furnace. Scientists study it to learn how a planet's air can completely change its climate.
summary: Mercury sits closer to the Sun — but Venus is hot enough to melt lead.

headline: A Day on Venus Is Longer Than a Year
body: Venus spins incredibly slowly. One full turn takes about 243 Earth days. But Venus circles the Sun in just 225 Earth days. That means a single day on Venus lasts longer than its entire year. No other planet has this strange mismatch between its day and its year.
summary: It spins so slowly that sunrise to sunrise outlasts a full lap around the Sun.

headline: Venus Spins Backward
body: Most planets spin the same way, but Venus turns the opposite direction. If you stood on its surface, the Sun would rise in the west and set in the east. Scientists aren't sure why. One idea is that a giant crash long ago flipped the planet's spin. It's one of the strangest motions in the solar system.
summary: If you stood on Venus, the Sun would rise in the west.

headline: The Pressure on Venus Could Crush a Submarine
body: The air on Venus is incredibly heavy. At the surface, it presses down more than 90 times harder than Earth's air. That's like being nearly a mile under the ocean. Mixed with the extreme heat, it makes Venus one of the deadliest places in the solar system. Most landers have survived only a couple of hours there.
summary: Standing there would feel like being a mile under the ocean.

headline: Venus Has Clouds Made of Acid
body: Venus is wrapped in thick clouds made of sulfuric acid. They bounce back so much sunlight that Venus is one of the brightest things in our night sky. But those same clouds help make the planet deadly. Scientists still study them closely. A few have even wondered if tiny life could float high in the cloud tops.
summary: The clouds that make it shine are made of acid — and may hide microbes.

headline: Mars Is Entirely Inhabited by Robots
body: Mars is the only world we know that is run entirely by robots. No human has ever set foot there. Instead, rovers like Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, and Perseverance have explored it for decades. They take photos, study rocks, and hunt for clues about Mars's past. In a way, Mars is already inhabited — just not by people.
summary: No human has ever stood there — yet it's been busy with visitors for decades.

headline: Mars Used to Be Warmer and Wetter
body: Mars is cold and dry today, but it wasn't always. Rovers and orbiters have found dried-up riverbeds, old lakebeds, and minerals that only form in water. Billions of years ago, Mars likely had thicker air and a warmer climate with liquid water. Figuring out how it changed so much helps scientists understand how planets — including Earth — can transform.
summary: The dry red desert once had rivers and lakes.

headline: Mars Might Have Once Had Life
body: Is there life beyond Earth? Mars is one of the best places to look. Long ago it had lakes, rivers, and a friendlier climate. Now scientists scan its rocks and soil for chemical “fingerprints” that tiny microbes might leave behind. Nothing has been found yet. But every mission brings new clues.
summary: If those ancient lakes held microbes, we may still find the traces.

headline: A Rover Has Used AI to Drive on Another Planet
body: Perseverance was the first rover to use artificial intelligence to drive itself on another planet. Instead of waiting for every command from Earth, it can study the ground and choose its own route. That lets it travel farther and faster across rough land. It matters because messages between Earth and Mars take minutes to arrive. Future missions may rely on the same trick.
summary: It doesn't wait for Earth — it picks its own path.

headline: A One-Year Mission Lasted More Than Eleven Years
body: NASA's MAVEN spacecraft was built to study Mars for just one year. Instead, it has kept working for more than eleven. In that time, it helped explain how Mars slowly lost most of its air and turned into a cold desert. The bonus years gave scientists far more data than they ever expected.
summary: Built to last 12 months, it's still working after more than a decade.

headline: Asteroids Are Older Than Earth
body: Asteroids are leftovers from the birth of the solar system, about 4.6 billion years ago. They formed from the same cloud of gas and dust as the Sun and planets, but never grew into worlds. Because they've barely changed since then, they hold clues about how everything began. Scientists call them time capsules from before Earth existed.
summary: These space rocks were around before our planet even formed.

headline: All the Asteroids Together Weigh Less Than the Moon
body: There are millions of asteroids in the solar system, but together they don't add up to much. If you squished every known asteroid into one ball, it would still weigh less than our Moon. Most of that weight sits in just a few big ones, like Ceres and Vesta. It shows just how empty space really is.
summary: Millions of space rocks, and together they still can't outweigh the Moon.

headline: Humans Have Successfully Moved an Asteroid
body: In 2022, NASA crashed a spacecraft into a small asteroid called Dimorphos on purpose. It worked: the asteroid's path actually changed. It was the first time humans ever moved an object in space. The mission, called DART, was a test of how we might protect Earth from a dangerous asteroid one day. It was a huge milestone.
summary: In 2022, we crashed a spacecraft into one on purpose — and it worked.

headline: NASA Has a Planetary Defense Office
body: NASA really does have a Planetary Defense office. Its job is to find and track space rocks that could come near Earth. Using powerful telescopes and computer models, scientists map out asteroid paths years or even decades ahead. The good news: no large asteroid is on a collision course right now. Spotting threats early would give us time to act.
summary: There's a real team whose job is watching the skies for incoming rocks.

headline: One Asteroid Completely Surprised NASA
body: When NASA's OSIRIS-REx reached the asteroid Bennu, scientists expected a smooth surface. Instead, they found it covered in jagged rocks and boulders. The surprise forced engineers to rethink how the spacecraft would grab a sample. Bennu was a good reminder that even small space rocks can catch experts off guard.
summary: NASA expected a smooth surface. It found a boulder-covered mess.

headline: There Are More Planets Than Stars in Our Galaxy
body: Planets are so common that the Milky Way may hold more planets than stars. Since our galaxy has hundreds of billions of stars, that's a staggering number of worlds. Scientists used to think planets might be rare. Now it looks like almost every star has them. Earth is just one planet among an enormous crowd.
summary: Our galaxy may hold more worlds than it has stars.

headline: We've Confirmed More Than 6,000 Alien Worlds
body: The first planet around a Sun-like star was found in the 1990s. Since then, scientists have confirmed more than 6,000 worlds beyond our solar system. They come in wild variety — some bigger than Jupiter, some about the size of Earth. And these are only the ones we've found so far. Many more are still out there waiting.
summary: And that's just the handful we've managed to spot so far.

headline: Some Planets Wander Through Space Alone
body: Not every planet circles a star. Some, called rogue planets, drift alone through space with no sun at all. Scientists think many were flung out of their original solar systems long ago. With no nearby star, these worlds get no warmth or light. They're among the strangest, darkest places we can imagine.
summary: No star, no sunrise — just endless dark.

headline: Scientists Have Found Lava Worlds and Styrofoam Worlds
body: Alien planets are far stranger than anyone guessed. Scientists have found worlds with oceans of molten rock, planets made mostly of iron, and one so light it's compared to Styrofoam. Some race around their star in just a few hours. Others drift alone in the dark. The universe is full of worlds that stretch the imagination.
summary: Some are oceans of lava; one is so light it's compared to Styrofoam.

headline: One Star Has Seven Earth-Sized Planets
body: A star called TRAPPIST-1 has seven planets, all about the size of Earth. Several sit in the “just right” zone where water could stay liquid — exactly where life might be possible. It's about 40 light-years away and one of the most studied star systems we know. It shows that solar systems can look very different from ours.
summary: And several sit in the zone where water could stay liquid.

headline: A Comet's Glowing Head Can Become Bigger Than a Planet
body: As a comet nears the Sun, its frozen gases heat up and burst out as a glowing cloud called a coma. The solid core can be small, but the coma around it can swell bigger than many planets. The Sun's heat drives the whole show. These giant glowing heads are why comets have amazed people for thousands of years.
summary: Heat puffs its frozen core into a glowing cloud wider than a planet.

headline: Comet Tails Can Stretch for Millions of Miles
body: A comet's tail forms when sunlight and solar particles blow gas and dust off its core. Strangely, the tail always points away from the Sun, not behind the comet. It can stretch for millions of miles. Yet it's so thin and empty that it holds almost nothing. These glowing streaks have inspired myths for ages.
summary: It always points away from the Sun — never trailing behind.

headline: A Comet Was Seen Reversing Its Spin for the First Time
body: Using the Hubble Telescope, astronomers spotted the first comet known to slow its spin and then reverse direction. They think jets of gas shooting from its surface gradually changed how it turned. It showed that comets aren't dead rocks — they're active and can change in surprising ways.
summary: Jets of gas slowly spun it down, then turned it the other way.

headline: A Comet Once Crashed Into Jupiter
body: In 1994, people watched a comet named Shoemaker-Levy 9 smash into Jupiter. The impacts left dark scars in Jupiter's clouds that were visible through telescopes on Earth. It was one of the most dramatic events ever seen in the solar system. It also proved that big collisions still happen today.
summary: In 1994, the world watched the scars appear from Earth.

headline: 'Oumuamua Was Humanity's First Known Interstellar Visitor
body: In 2017, scientists spotted ‘Oumuamua — the first object ever seen passing through our solar system from another star. Its odd shape and strange motion sparked huge debate. Unlike normal comets, it came from far beyond our Sun and had traveled millions of years to reach us. It opened a whole new field: studying visitors from other star systems.
summary: It spent millions of years crossing space before passing through our system.

headline: Scientists Think Hundreds More Dwarf Planets Exist
body: Only five dwarf planets are officially recognized, but scientists think hundreds more are out there. Many likely orbit far past Neptune, in an icy zone called the Kuiper Belt. They're hard to spot because they're small and incredibly far away. As telescopes improve, many more should turn up.
summary: Only five are official — but hundreds may be hiding past Neptune.

headline: Haumea Is One of the Fastest-Spinning Worlds Known
body: Haumea is one of the strangest worlds out there. It spins so fast that it's been stretched into an oval, like an egg, instead of a ball. One full turn takes only about four hours. That makes it one of the fastest-spinning large objects we know. Its shape hints at a giant crash billions of years ago.
summary: It spins so fast it's been stretched into an egg shape.

headline: Pluto Lost Planet Status Because It Couldn't Clear Its Orbit
body: In 2006, scientists made an official rule: a planet has to sweep its orbit clear of other objects. Pluto couldn't — it shares its space with many icy bodies out past Neptune. So it was renamed a dwarf planet. The choice upset a lot of people and is still argued about today.
summary: It still shares its lane with crowds of icy objects.

headline: Eris Helped Redefine What a Planet Is
body: When Eris was discovered in 2005, it raised a tricky question. Eris is about the same size as Pluto — so if Pluto counted as a planet, shouldn't Eris too? Instead of adding a planet, scientists made a brand-new group: dwarf planets. That decision is what bumped Pluto out of the planet club a year later.
summary: Finding a world Pluto's size forced a hard question.

headline: The Official Planet Definition Didn't Exist Until 2006
body: It sounds odd, but for most of history there was no exact definition of a planet. That changed in 2006, when astronomers finally set official rules. The same rules created the dwarf planet group and changed how we sort worlds. It's proof that even a familiar word like “planet” can change as science learns more.
summary: For most of history, nobody had agreed on what 'planet' even meant.

headline: One Thousand Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter
body: Jupiter is by far the biggest planet in our solar system. If it were hollow, about 1,000 Earths could fit inside. Yet it's not solid — it's made mostly of hydrogen and helium, like the Sun. Its huge gravity tugs on asteroids and comets all around it. Studying Jupiter helps scientists learn how giant planets form.
summary: Our whole planet would be a marble inside it.

headline: Jupiter Is More Massive Than All the Other Planets Combined
body: Jupiter is more than twice as heavy as all the other planets combined. That enormous mass gives it powerful gravity. Scientists think it formed early and helped shape where the other planets ended up. Its pull also grabs or flings away passing comets and asteroids. Without Jupiter, our solar system might look very different.
summary: Add up every other planet, and Jupiter still wins — twice over.

headline: Jupiter Has the Shortest Day in the Solar System
body: Jupiter is huge, but it spins incredibly fast. One full day there lasts only about 10 hours — the shortest of any planet. That speed makes Jupiter bulge out at its middle and helps create its famous striped clouds. It also powers the planet's strong magnetic field. Sunrises come fast on Jupiter.
summary: The biggest planet also spins the fastest — a day is under 10 hours.

headline: Jupiter Has 95 Officially Recognized Moons
body: Jupiter has one of the biggest moon families in the solar system — 95 recognized so far. They range from tiny lumps of rock to giant moons bigger than the planet Mercury. A few, like Europa and Ganymede, are top targets for future missions. Together, Jupiter and its moons act like a mini solar system.
summary: One of them is bigger than the planet Mercury.

headline: Jupiter's Great Red Spot Is a Giant Storm
body: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a giant storm — big enough to swallow the entire Earth. It has been spinning for centuries, far longer than any storm on our planet. Scientists still wonder why it lasts so long and why it's slowly shrinking. It shows that weather on other worlds can be wilder than anything here.
summary: A storm so big it could swallow Earth — and it's raged for centuries.

headline: Saturn Would Float in Water
body: Saturn is the lightest planet for its size. It's so spread out that, if you had a big enough bathtub, Saturn would actually float. That's because it's a giant ball of hydrogen and helium gas, not solid rock. It's a fun way to picture just how different gas giants are from rocky worlds like Earth.
summary: Find a big enough bathtub and the whole planet would bob on top.

headline: One of Saturn's Moons Has Lakes and Rain
body: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is the only place besides Earth with lakes and rivers on its surface. But they're not water — they're liquid methane and ethane. Titan even has clouds and rain, with weather cycles a bit like ours. Despite its freezing cold, scientists call it one of the most Earth-like spots in the solar system.
summary: But the rain isn't water — it's liquid methane.

headline: A Small Moon May Hide an Ocean Beneath Its Ice
body: Saturn's little moon Enceladus seems to hide a whole ocean under its icy shell. Spacecraft spotted giant geysers spraying water out into space, likely fed by that hidden sea. Since life needs water, Enceladus is now one of the best places to search for it. A tiny frozen moon may hold a big secret.
summary: Geysers blast its hidden sea straight out into space.

headline: NASA Ended Cassini With a Controlled Crash
body: After more than 13 years exploring Saturn, NASA flew its Cassini spacecraft straight into the planet in 2017. Why crash it on purpose? To make sure it never drifted into moons like Enceladus or Titan and contaminated them. Along the way, Cassini sent back stunning images and found signs of hidden oceans. Many call it one of the greatest space missions ever.
summary: After 13 years, NASA flew it straight into the planet — on purpose.

headline: Saturn's Winds Are Faster Than Earth's Strongest Hurricanes
body: Saturn has some of the fiercest weather around. Winds near its equator can blow over 1,000 miles per hour — far faster than the strongest hurricane on Earth. Those winds shape the planet's banded clouds. Scientists study them to learn how giant planets work. It's a reminder that weather out there can be far more violent than ours.
summary: Winds there top 1,000 mph — Earth's worst storms can't compare.

headline: Uranus Rotates on Its Side
body: Uranus is tipped over almost completely — about 98 degrees — so it rolls around the Sun on its side. Scientists think a massive crash early on knocked it over. Because of the tilt, its seasons are extreme: one pole can face the Sun for years while the other sits in darkness. It's one of the oddest planets we've found.
summary: It rolls around the Sun like a tipped-over ball.

headline: Uranus Is the Coldest Planet in the Solar System
body: Neptune is farther from the Sun, but Uranus is actually the coldest planet. Temperatures can drop to around -371°F. Strangely, Uranus gives off almost no heat of its own, unlike the other giant planets. Scientists still don't know why. It's one of the biggest mysteries about this icy world.
summary: Neptune is farther out, yet Uranus is the colder one.

headline: Uranus Was the First Planet Discovered With a Telescope
body: For thousands of years, people only knew the planets they could see with their eyes. Then in 1781, William Herschel spotted Uranus through a telescope. At first he thought it was a comet, until others realized it was a whole new planet. The find pushed the edge of the known solar system farther out than ever before.
summary: For all of history before 1781, no one knew it was there.

headline: A Season on Uranus Can Last More Than 20 Years
body: Uranus is tipped on its side and takes 84 Earth years to circle the Sun. That makes each season last about 21 years. For all that time, one half of the planet can bask in sunlight while the other half sits in darkness. These super-long seasons are unlike anything on Earth.
summary: Imagine 21 straight years of summer — or darkness.

headline: Only One Spacecraft Has Ever Visited Uranus
body: Only one spacecraft has ever visited Uranus: NASA's Voyager 2, which flew past in 1986. That single visit gave us our only close-up photos of the planet and its moons. No mission has returned since, so many mysteries remain. Even though it's a major planet, Uranus is still mostly unexplored.
summary: Our only close-up came during a single flyby in 1986.

headline: Pluto Has a Giant Heart on Its Surface
body: When NASA's New Horizons flew past Pluto in 2015, it spotted something amazing: a giant, heart-shaped region on the surface. Scientists named it Tombaugh Regio, and it's now one of the most famous sights in the solar system. The “heart” is filled with frozen nitrogen that slowly flows and reshapes the land. Pluto went from a fuzzy dot to a living world.
summary: A spacecraft revealed a heart of frozen ice, miles wide.

headline: Pluto Has a Blue Sky
body: One of New Horizons' biggest surprises was Pluto's blue sky. Sunlight hitting tiny haze particles high above the surface gives the sky its color — a bit like on Earth. That's stunning for a world so far from the Sun. It showed that even tiny, frozen places can have surprisingly rich atmospheres.
summary: Billions of miles from the Sun, its sky still glows blue.

headline: Pluto Has Mountains Made of Ice
body: New Horizons found towering mountains on Pluto, thousands of feet tall. But they aren't made of rock — they're made of water ice. Pluto is so cold that ice gets as hard as stone. Some of the mountains look young, which means Pluto is still active inside. That shocked scientists who expected a dead, frozen world.
summary: They're as hard as rock — because they're frozen that solid.

headline: Pluto and Charon Form an Unusual Pair
body: Pluto's moon Charon is so large compared to Pluto that the two actually orbit a point in space between them. Because of this, some scientists call them a “double” system. They're also locked together, always showing each other the same face — like two dancers spinning as a pair. Nothing else in the solar system is quite like them.
summary: Its moon is so big they orbit a point in empty space between them.

headline: A Year on Pluto Lasts Nearly 250 Earth Years
body: Pluto is so far from the Sun that one trip around takes about 248 Earth years. We discovered Pluto in 1930 — and it still hasn't finished a single orbit since. Its seasons stretch over decades. The huge year shows just how enormous the solar system really is.
summary: Since we found it in 1930, it still hasn't finished one lap.

headline: Our Solar System Is Traveling Around the Milky Way
body: The planets orbit the Sun — but the whole solar system is also flying through the Milky Way. The Sun and everything around it circle the galaxy's center at hundreds of thousands of miles per hour. This has gone on for billions of years. So even sitting still on Earth, you're a passenger on a giant cosmic ride.
summary: Even sitting still, you're racing around the galaxy.

headline: One Trip Around the Galaxy Takes 230 Million Years
body: It takes our solar system about 230 million years to loop once around the Milky Way. Scientists call that a “galactic year.” When dinosaurs were alive, the Sun sat in a totally different part of the galaxy. In all of Earth's history, we've made only about 20 of these loops. It shows how mind-bendingly big the galaxy is.
summary: Back when dinosaurs roamed, we were on the other side of the galaxy.

headline: More Than 300 Spacecraft Have Left Earth
body: Since the Space Age began, we've launched hundreds of robotic spacecraft into the solar system. They've reached every major planet, plus moons, asteroids, and comets. Missions like Voyager, Cassini, and New Horizons revealed worlds stranger than anyone imagined. These robots are our eyes and hands in places no human can yet go.
summary: Robots have reached every planet so we don't have to.

headline: Only 24 Humans Have Ever Traveled to the Moon
body: Only 24 people have ever traveled to the Moon, all during NASA's Apollo missions between 1968 and 1972. Of those, just 12 actually walked on the surface. Since the last trip in 1972, no one has gone back — or even left low Earth orbit. It's still one of humanity's greatest achievements.
summary: And just 12 of them actually walked on it.

headline: Earth Is Still the Only World Known to Host Life
body: Out of every planet, moon, and space rock we know, Earth is still the only place with confirmed life. Scientists keep searching spots like Mars, Europa, and Enceladus for signs of it. Some of those worlds could support life — but nothing has been found yet. Why Earth came alive is one of the biggest questions in science.
summary: After searching dozens of worlds, ours is still the only living one.

headline: The Sun Holds the Solar System Together
body: The Sun holds more than 99% of all the mass in the solar system. That gives it an enormous gravitational pull. It's this pull that keeps the planets, moons, asteroids, and comets circling in place. Without it, the solar system would simply fly apart. The Sun is the anchor that holds everything together.
summary: It holds 99% of everything — and its grip keeps us all in line.

headline: Billions of Stars Are Similar to Our Sun
body: The Sun feels one of a kind, but it's actually pretty ordinary. It's a yellow dwarf star, and the Milky Way holds billions of stars about the same size and temperature. Studying these “twins” helps scientists learn how stars are born, live, and die. Many of them likely have planets too.
summary: Our Sun is special to us — but the galaxy is full of stars just like it.

headline: Ordinary People Can Help Discover Comets
body: You don't need to be a scientist to discover a comet. NASA's Sungrazer Project lets volunteers study images from Sun-watching telescopes. Regular people have already helped find thousands of comets this way. It's one of the most successful “citizen science” projects ever. All it takes is curiosity and a sharp eye.
summary: Volunteers at home have already found thousands of comets.

headline: Some Comets Die in the Sun
body: Some comets fly so close to the Sun that they don't survive. These “sungrazers” can break apart or boil away in the Sun's intense heat. Sun-watching telescopes have caught these dramatic endings on camera. Studying them helps scientists learn what comets are made of — and produces some breathtaking views.
summary: Fly too close, and the Sun vaporizes them completely.

headline: The Sun Helps Create Earth's Auroras
body: The glowing northern and southern lights are powered by the Sun. It sends out streams of tiny charged particles that crash into Earth's magnetic field and air, lighting up the sky near the poles. This is part of “space weather,” which can even affect satellites. The auroras are a beautiful sign of how connected Earth and the Sun really are.
summary: The northern lights aren't really about the poles — they start at the Sun.

headline: Neptune May Have Stopped a Planet From Forming
body: The Kuiper Belt is full of icy leftovers from the early solar system. NASA says these objects might have come together to form a planet if Neptune had not been there. Neptune's gravity stirred the region so much that the small icy bodies could not merge into one large world. Instead, they stayed scattered beyond Neptune, leaving behind a frozen zone of almost-planets.
summary: A whole planet may have failed to form because Neptune kept stirring the pieces apart.

headline: The Kuiper Belt Is Huge but Barely Weighs Anything
body: The Kuiper Belt is one of the largest structures in our solar system. It begins near Neptune's orbit, around 30 AU from the Sun, and its main region reaches about 50 AU. A second region called the scattered disk stretches much farther, nearly to 1,000 AU. Even with all that space, NASA estimates the belt's total material is no more than about 10% of Earth's mass.
summary: It stretches far beyond Neptune, but all its material adds up to only a small slice of Earth.

headline: Pluto Crosses Neptune's Orbit but Avoids Neptune
body: Pluto's path crosses Neptune's orbit, but the two worlds do not have close encounters. Pluto is locked in a stable pattern with Neptune called a resonance. For every three trips Neptune makes around the Sun, Pluto makes two. NASA says this keeps Pluto from getting close enough for Neptune's gravity to bother it much. Pluto even gets physically closer to Uranus than it ever does to Neptune.
summary: Pluto crosses Neptune's orbit, yet its timing keeps it safely out of Neptune's way.

headline: A Spacecraft Visited a Red Snowman World
body: Arrokoth is a small Kuiper Belt object made of two lobes that touch. NASA calls this kind of object a contact binary. When New Horizons flew past it on Jan. 1, 2019, the images showed something like a partly flattened red snowman. NASA says Arrokoth is the most distant and most primitive object ever explored by a spacecraft.
summary: New Horizons found a tiny double-lobed world that looked like a flattened red snowman.

headline: Voyager 1 Still Has 300 Years to Reach the Oort Cloud
body: The Oort Cloud is so far away that even Voyager 1 is nowhere near it yet. NASA says Voyager 1 travels about a million miles a day. At that speed, it will not enter the Oort Cloud for about 300 years. It may take another 30,000 years before the spacecraft exits the cloud's outer edge.
summary: The farthest spacecraft from Earth still has centuries to go before it reaches this icy frontier.

headline: Sunlight Takes Days to Reach the Oort Cloud
body: Sunlight reaches Earth in a little over eight minutes. It reaches Neptune's orbit in about 4.5 hours, then passes beyond the Kuiper Belt a few hours later. But NASA says that same sunlight needs another 10 to 28 days to reach the inner edge of the Oort Cloud. It could take as much as a year and a half to pass beyond the cloud's outer edge.
summary: Light can cross the inner solar system in hours, then spend days reaching this distant shell.

headline: The Oort Cloud Is a Bubble Not a Belt
body: The Oort Cloud is not shaped like a flat ring around the Sun. NASA says its objects can travel over, under, and at many angles around the solar system. That makes it more like a thick bubble of icy debris than a belt. This is also why long-period comets can seem to arrive from all directions, instead of from the same plane as the planets.
summary: It surrounds the solar system like a giant icy bubble, which helps explain why some comets come from every direction.

headline: Some Comets Last Visited Before Humans Existed
body: The Oort Cloud may hold hundreds of billions, or even trillions, of icy bodies. Sometimes one is disturbed and begins a long fall toward the Sun as a comet. NASA says most known long-period comets have been seen only once in recorded history because their orbits are so long. Some last passed through the inner solar system before our species existed. Others may never have come near the Sun since they formed.
summary: Some comets take so long to return that humans were not around the last time they visited.

headline: Meteor Showers Are Earth Flying Through Comet Dust
body: A meteor shower happens when Earth passes through a trail of dusty debris left by a comet. Those tiny bits rush into our atmosphere at high speed and burn up as meteors. NASA says most of this comet debris is only between the size of a grain of sand and a pea. So a night full of shooting stars can come from specks of old comet dust.
summary: Those bright streaks in the sky can come from dust no bigger than sand or a pea.

headline: Every Perseid Meteor Is a Piece of One Comet
body: The Perseids are one of the most famous meteor showers, and they peak around August 12 each year. NASA says every Perseid meteor is a tiny piece of comet Swift-Tuttle. That comet swings by the Sun every 135 years, leaving a dusty trail behind it. When Earth crosses that trail, pieces of the comet burn up above us as bright streaks.
summary: A summer meteor shower is really Earth crossing the leftovers of comet Swift-Tuttle.

headline: Meteor Showers Usually Don't Drop Meteorites
body: Meteor showers look dramatic, but they usually do not leave space rocks on the ground. NASA says most meteor showers come from comets, and comet material is fragile. Small comet fragments generally burn up before they can survive the trip through Earth's atmosphere. The Taurids and Geminids could, in theory, send meteorites down sometimes, but no remnants have been clearly traced to them.
summary: A shower can fill the sky with meteors and still leave nothing to pick up afterward.

headline: Earth Gets Tons of Space Material Every Day
body: Space is not as empty as it looks from the ground. NASA says about 48.5 tons, or 44,000 kilograms, of meteoritic material falls on Earth each day. Much of it burns up in the atmosphere as meteors before anyone can notice it. Sometimes a meteor can even appear brighter than Venus, and that extra-bright streak is called a fireball.
summary: Earth is quietly collecting space material every day, even when no meteor shower is happening.

headline: Voyager 2 Launched Before Voyager 1
body: The names make it sound like Voyager 1 should have launched first, but it didn't. NASA says Voyager 2 launched from Cape Canaveral on Aug. 20, 1977. Voyager 1 launched later, on Sept. 5, using a similar rocket. The two spacecraft became twins in one of the most famous space missions ever, even though the second-numbered Voyager left Earth first.
summary: The spacecraft called Voyager 2 actually left Earth before Voyager 1.

headline: Voyager Visited Every Giant Planet
body: Voyager was not just a trip to one faraway world. NASA says Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 explored all the giant planets in our solar system. Between them, they studied Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune, 48 moons, and the rings and magnetic fields around those worlds. Voyager 2 even became the first spacecraft to fly by Neptune in the summer of 1989.
summary: Two spacecraft gave humanity its first grand tour of the solar system's giant planets.

headline: Voyager 1 Became the Most Distant Human-Made Object
body: Voyager 1 is one of humanity's farthest-reaching machines. NASA says that on Feb. 17, 1998, it passed Pioneer 10 and became the most distant human-made object in space. That means a spacecraft launched in 1977 eventually moved farther away than anything people had ever built before. It kept going long after its original planetary tour was over.
summary: A 1977 spacecraft became the farthest human-made object ever sent into space.

headline: Both Voyagers Reached Interstellar Space
body: The Voyager spacecraft did not stop after visiting the outer planets. NASA says Voyager 1 entered interstellar space on Aug. 25, 2012. Voyager 2 reached interstellar space on Nov. 5, 2018. They are traveling away from the plane of the planets in different directions, carrying their mission beyond the region dominated by the Sun.
summary: Both Voyager spacecraft have crossed into the space between stars.

headline: One Moon Has Hundreds of Active Volcanoes
body: Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanic world in the solar system. Jupiter and its other moons constantly squeeze and stretch Io, heating its insides until hundreds of volcanoes erupt. Some blasts fling material hundreds of miles into space. Its surface is always changing, painted in yellow sulfur. Io proves moons can be wild, active places.
summary: Some eruptions shoot material hundreds of miles into space.

headline: A Moon May Hide More Water Than Earth
body: Under the icy crust of Jupiter's moon Europa lies a giant ocean — possibly holding more water than all of Earth's oceans combined. Scientists found hints of it from spacecraft and Europa's magnetic field. Since life needs water, Europa is a top place to search for it. Future missions hope to explore that hidden sea.
summary: Under its ice may sit more water than all of Earth's oceans.

headline: One Moon Has Thicker Air Than Earth
body: Saturn's moon Titan has a thick atmosphere — even thicker than Earth's. It's mostly nitrogen, and it has clouds, rain, and lakes, though they're made of methane, not water. Titan is the only moon with stable liquids on its surface. Scientists see it as one of the most Earth-like places beyond our planet.
summary: It even has clouds, rain, and lakes — just not made of water.

headline: The Largest Moon Is Bigger Than Mercury
body: Jupiter's moon Ganymede is the biggest moon in the solar system — even larger than the planet Mercury. It still counts as a moon because it orbits Jupiter, not the Sun. Ganymede even has its own magnetic field and may hide an ocean under its ice. Its size blurs the line between moon and planet.
summary: If it orbited the Sun, we might call it a planet.

headline: A Tiny Moon Shoots Water Into Space
body: Saturn's small moon Enceladus shoots huge plumes of water and ice into space through cracks near its south pole. Those geysers point to a hidden ocean beneath its icy shell. Spacecraft even flew through the spray and found ingredients important for life. That makes this tiny moon one of the best places to search for it.
summary: Spacecraft flew through its spray and found life's ingredients.

headline: Not Even Light Can Escape a Black Hole
body: A black hole's gravity is so strong that once something crosses the edge — called the event horizon — it can never get back out. Even light, the fastest thing in the universe, gets trapped. That's why a black hole looks completely dark. Scientists can't see them directly, so instead they watch how nearby stars and gas behave.
summary: Cross the edge and there's no coming back — not even for light.

headline: Spaghettification Is a Real Scientific Term
body: If you fell toward certain black holes, scientists say you'd be “spaghettified.” Near a black hole, gravity pulls much harder on the closer side of an object than the far side. That stretches it long and thin while squeezing it narrow — like a strand of spaghetti. The funny name describes a very real effect. It's one of the strangest predictions in Einstein's theory of gravity.
summary: Get too close and gravity stretches you into a strand of pasta.

headline: Some Black Holes Spin More Than 1,000 Times Every Second
body: Black holes don't just sit still — they spin, and some spin shockingly fast. One called GRS 1915+105 turns more than 1,000 times every second, making it one of the fastest-spinning objects known. That whirling motion stirs up the gas and magnetic fields around it. It may even help fire off giant jets of material into space.
summary: One whips around more than 1,000 times every single second.

headline: Black Holes Aren't Cosmic Vacuum Cleaners
body: Movies show black holes sucking in everything nearby, but that's not how they work. From a safe distance, a black hole pulls just like any object with the same mass. If the Sun suddenly became a black hole of equal mass, Earth's orbit wouldn't change. You'd only get trapped if you flew very close to the edge. Otherwise, they're no more dangerous than a regular star.
summary: From a safe distance, one pulls no harder than a normal star.

headline: Earth Would Keep Orbiting a Black Hole Sun
body: Many people think the solar system would get swallowed if the Sun became a black hole. It wouldn't. As long as the black hole had the same mass as the Sun, the planets would keep orbiting like normal. Gravity depends on mass, not on whether something is a star or a black hole. The real problem would be losing light and heat. Earth would freeze — but it wouldn't fall in.
summary: Swap the Sun for a black hole of equal mass and we'd keep circling — just freeze.

headline: Some Galaxies Contain Trillions of Stars
body: Galaxies vary enormously in size. Small “dwarf” galaxies may hold only a few thousand stars, while the biggest can hold trillions. Each one is a giant collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter held together by gravity. Some stretch hundreds of thousands of light-years across. By comparing big and small galaxies, scientists learn how the universe grew up.
summary: From a few thousand stars to trillions — galaxies come in wild sizes.

headline: Most Large Galaxies Hide Giant Black Holes
body: Most large galaxies hide a supermassive black hole right at their center. These giants can weigh millions or even billions of times more than the Sun. Scientists found them by watching stars and gas whip around an invisible point. These black holes seem to shape how their galaxies grow. Even our Milky Way has one, called Sagittarius A*.
summary: Including ours — a giant one sits at the heart of the Milky Way.

headline: Some Galaxies Are Almost as Old as the Universe
body: Some galaxies formed amazingly early — only a few hundred million years after the Big Bang. Because their light takes so long to reach us, we see them as they looked back then, not today. Studying them is like looking at baby pictures of the universe. They're among the oldest things humans have ever seen.
summary: We see them as they looked just after the universe began.

headline: Galaxies Form a Giant Cosmic Web
body: Galaxies aren't sprinkled randomly through space. They line up along giant threads of matter called the cosmic web. These strands connect clusters of galaxies, with huge empty voids in between. Mapping this web shows how gravity has shaped the universe since the Big Bang. It's one of the largest patterns we know of.
summary: Galaxies aren't scattered randomly — they hang on invisible threads.

headline: Some Galaxies Have Only a Few Thousand Stars
body: Not all galaxies are giants. Some “dwarf” galaxies hold only a few thousand stars — tiny next to the Milky Way. But they're useful, because they hold clues about the early universe. In fact, big galaxies often grow by swallowing smaller ones over billions of years. These mini-galaxies help scientists trace cosmic history.
summary: Next to the Milky Way, some galaxies are practically tiny.

headline: There May Be One Septillion Stars in the Universe
body: Astronomers estimate the visible universe holds about one septillion stars — a 1 followed by 24 zeros. They get this number by counting galaxies and figuring out how many stars each one holds. It's almost impossible to picture. And that's only the part we can see. The real total could be even higher.
summary: A 1 with 24 zeros after it — and that's just the part we can see.

headline: Some Stars Can Live for Trillions of Years
body: Small red dwarf stars burn their fuel very slowly, so they can last for trillions of years. That's far longer than the universe's current age of about 13.8 billion years. In fact, the universe is so young that no red dwarf has died yet. These stars may end up being the last ones still shining in the far future.
summary: The universe is too young for any of them to have died yet.

headline: The Atoms in Your Body Were Made Inside Stars
body: Stars work like giant furnaces, building many of the elements in the universe. Carbon, oxygen, iron, and more were all made inside stars and then scattered into space. That material later became planets, oceans, and even living things. Scientists like to say we're made of stardust — because the atoms in your body really did come from ancient stars.
summary: Carbon, oxygen, iron — forged in stars long before Earth existed.

headline: Some Stars Die in Giant Explosions
body: When a massive star runs out of fuel, it can blow apart in a supernova. For a short time, the explosion can outshine an entire galaxy. It also flings newly made elements across space, seeding future stars and planets. Some of the atoms in Earth — and in you — were made in blasts like these.
summary: For a few weeks, one dying star can outshine a whole galaxy.

headline: Dead Stars Can Become Black Holes
body: The most massive stars don't fade quietly. When they run out of fuel, they can collapse under their own gravity and form a black hole. These objects are so dense that not even light escapes them. Scientists spot them by watching how nearby stars and gas move. A dying star can leave behind one of the strangest things in the universe.
summary: The biggest stars don't fade out — they collapse into something darker.

headline: The Stars in a Constellation Aren't Actually Connected
body: Stars in a constellation look close together, but they usually aren't. Many sit at wildly different distances and just happen to line up from our view on Earth. Ancient people connected them into pictures and stories, creating the constellations we still use. Today they work as handy maps of the sky — not real groups of stars.
summary: Stars that look side by side can be trillions of miles apart.

headline: Different Cultures Invented Different Constellations
body: People everywhere have looked at the night sky and seen different things. Ancient Greeks, Chinese astronomers, and Indigenous cultures all made their own constellations from the same stars. Each one reflected the animals, myths, and beliefs that mattered to them. Modern astronomy uses 88 official constellations, but many older ones are still celebrated. The stars have always inspired both science and stories.
summary: The same stars became different pictures all around the world.

headline: Meteor Showers Are Named After Constellations
body: Meteor showers are named after the constellation they seem to shoot out from. The Perseids, for example, appear to come from the constellation Perseus. But the meteors are really bits of debris left behind by comets, which Earth plows through each year. The constellation is just a handy signpost. These yearly shows have amazed skywatchers for centuries.
summary: The Perseids 'come from' Perseus — but really from a comet's trail.

headline: NASA Astronauts Still Train to Navigate by the Stars
body: Even with high-tech computers, astronauts still learn to navigate by the stars. It's a backup in case the electronic systems fail during a mission. By spotting certain stars and constellations, they can tell which way they're facing in space. Sailors and explorers used the same trick for centuries. Ancient sky knowledge still comes in handy in the age of spaceflight.
summary: If the computers fail, the stars are the backup plan.

headline: Spacecraft Use Star Maps to Find Their Way
body: Many spacecraft carry “star trackers” — cameras that compare the sky to a stored star map. By matching familiar patterns, they figure out exactly which way the spacecraft is facing. That lets telescopes and probes aim precisely at their targets. It works because stars barely move over a human lifetime. Even advanced spacecraft still rely on the night sky to find their way.
summary: Tiny 'star trackers' match the sky to a map to stay pointed right.

headline: Some Nebulae Are Giant Star Nurseries
body: Many nebulae are giant clouds of gas and dust where new stars are being born. Gravity pulls the material into clumps that slowly grow into young stars. Space telescopes have caught stunning photos of these “star nurseries” at work. Our own Sun probably formed inside a cloud like this billions of years ago. They're key places in the cosmic cycle of creation.
summary: Our own Sun was likely born inside a cloud like this.

headline: Some Nebulae Are the Remains of Dead Stars
body: Not every nebula makes stars — some are made by dying ones. When an old star sheds its outer layers, or a giant star explodes, it leaves an expanding cloud of gas and dust. These clouds carry heavy elements that may someday form new stars and planets. The Crab Nebula and Ring Nebula are famous examples. In space, a star's death helps fuel the next generation.
summary: Some glowing clouds are what a star leaves behind when it dies.

headline: Some Nebulae Look Like Giant Cosmic Mountains
body: Telescopes like Hubble and James Webb have photographed towering shapes inside nebulae that look like mountains, cliffs, and valleys. They're carved by radiation and winds from nearby stars pushing on clouds of gas and dust. They look solid, but they're actually incredibly thin and spread across huge distances. These dramatic scenes are some of the most famous images in astronomy.
summary: Those 'cliffs' in space photos are gas so thin you'd never feel them.

headline: One Nebula Looks Like a Giant Eyeball
body: The Helix Nebula is nicknamed “The Eye of God” because it looks like a giant eye staring out of space. It formed when a dying star puffed off its outer layers. The colorful rings in photos are glowing gas lit up by the hot remains of the star at the center. Its strange shape makes it one of the most recognizable objects in the sky.
summary: It's nicknamed 'The Eye of God' for a reason.

headline: Webb Can See Baby Stars Hidden Inside Nebulae
body: The James Webb Space Telescope sees infrared light — a kind of heat glow — so it can peer through dust that blocks normal light. That lets it spot baby stars hidden deep inside nebulae that were invisible before. Studying them shows how stars and planets first take shape. Webb has captured some of the clearest views ever of these hidden star nurseries.
summary: It sees heat, so it peeks right through the dust that hides newborn stars.

headline: Some Supernovas Outshine Entire Galaxies
body: When a giant star explodes as a supernova, it can briefly shine brighter than every star in its galaxy combined. The blast releases so much energy it can be seen across the universe. The dazzling light may last only weeks, but the wreckage spreads for millions of years. Astronomers study supernovas to learn how stars live and die. They're some of the most spectacular events in the universe.
summary: For a few weeks, one exploding star outshines billions of others.

headline: Supernovas Help Create the Ingredients for Life
body: Many of the elements on Earth — and inside you — were made in stars and blasted out by supernovas. These explosions spread carbon, oxygen, iron, and other heavy elements across the galaxy. Over time, that material becomes new stars, planets, and living things. It's why scientists say we're made of stardust. Without supernovas, life as we know it couldn't exist.
summary: Every explosion scatters the very atoms life is built from.

headline: Some Supernovas Leave Behind Neutron Stars
body: When some stars explode, their crushed cores survive as neutron stars. A neutron star can squeeze more mass than the Sun into a ball only about a dozen miles wide. That gives them incredibly strong gravity and magnetism, far beyond anything on Earth. Scientists study them to learn how matter behaves under the most extreme conditions. They're among the strangest leftovers of a star's death.
summary: Imagine the Sun's mass crushed into a city-sized ball.

headline: Ancient People Saw Supernovas in Daylight
body: A few supernovas in history were so bright they could be seen in daylight. Ancient astronomers in China, Japan, and elsewhere carefully wrote them down. Today, scientists use those old records to find the wreckage the explosions left behind. It's a neat link between ancient skywatchers and modern science. Humans have been witnessing exploding stars for thousands of years.
summary: Some exploding stars were bright enough to see in broad daylight.

headline: One Famous Supernova Created the Crab Nebula
body: In 1054, people across the world saw a brilliant new star appear in the sky. It was actually a supernova — an exploding star. Its glowing wreckage is now called the Crab Nebula. At its heart spins a fast neutron star, and scientists still study it closely. It's one of the most-watched objects in all of astronomy.
summary: A 'new star' people saw in 1054 was actually a star blowing up.

headline: Our Solar System Lives in the Galactic Suburbs
body: Instead of sitting near the busy center of the Milky Way, our solar system lives about halfway out, in a calmer area. It's a small spiral region called the Orion Spur. Some scientists think this quiet spot helped life develop safely on Earth. By studying nearby stars and gas, they keep learning about our galactic neighborhood. Even inside a galaxy, some areas are busier than others.
summary: We're not downtown — we live in a quiet corner of the galaxy.

headline: One Trip Around the Milky Way Takes 250 Million Years
body: The Sun and everything around it are slowly circling the center of the Milky Way. One full loop — a “galactic year” — takes about 250 million years. Back when dinosaurs lived, the solar system was in a totally different part of the galaxy. In all of Earth's history, we've made only about 20 of these loops. It shows just how huge and old the galaxy is.
summary: Since dinosaurs roamed, we haven't even finished one lap.

headline: Nobody Has Ever Photographed the Milky Way From Outside
body: We live inside the Milky Way, and no spacecraft has ever flown far enough to photograph it from outside. So how do we know its shape? Astronomers piece it together by mapping stars and gas, and by studying other galaxies that look like ours. Every picture of the Milky Way is really a careful reconstruction. It's like drawing a city while standing in the middle of it.
summary: Every 'photo' of our galaxy is really an educated guess.

headline: Another Galaxy Is Heading Straight Toward Us
body: Our biggest galactic neighbor, Andromeda, is slowly drifting toward the Milky Way. Astronomers know this by measuring how its light shifts as it moves. Even though the two are millions of light-years apart, gravity is pulling them together. It's happening so slowly that nothing big will change for billions of years. Even galaxies drift and meet over time.
summary: A whole galaxy is drifting our way — gravity is reeling it in.

headline: The Milky Way Will Collide With Andromeda
body: Scientists predict the Milky Way and Andromeda will merge billions of years from now. But “collision” is misleading: stars are so far apart that almost none will actually crash. Instead, gravity will slowly blend the two into one bigger galaxy. Computer models show the whole process takes billions of years. It's one of the most dramatic events in our galaxy's future.
summary: Two galaxies will merge — yet almost no stars will actually hit.

headline: Looking Into Space Is Looking Into the Past
body: Light takes time to travel, so we always see things as they were when their light left them — not as they are now. The Moon looks about a second old. The Sun, a few minutes. Distant galaxies can look billions of years old. By studying light from different distances, scientists read the history of the universe. Telescopes are basically time machines.
summary: The Moon you see is a second old; far galaxies, billions.

headline: The Sun We See Is Already Eight Minutes Old
body: Light is incredibly fast — about 186,000 miles per second — yet it still takes 8.3 minutes to reach us from the Sun. So the Sun you see is actually how it looked 8 minutes ago. If it suddenly disappeared, Earth would stay sunlit for those 8 minutes before going dark. Even our closest star is seen in the past.
summary: If the Sun vanished, we'd keep seeing it for 8 more minutes.

headline: Even Nearby Stars Are Years in the Past
body: The nearest star system after the Sun, Alpha Centauri, is more than four light-years away. So its light reaching us today actually left it over four years ago. If something dramatic happened there right now, we wouldn't find out for years. Even our closest neighbors in space are always seen in the past.
summary: The next star over is seen as it looked 4 years ago.

headline: Andromeda Appears 2.5 Million Years Younger
body: The Andromeda Galaxy is about 2.5 million light-years away. That means the light hitting our telescopes today started its trip before modern humans existed. Looking at Andromeda is like seeing a neighbor as they were millions of years ago. It helps scientists study how galaxies change. Looking out into space really is looking back in time.
summary: Its light left before modern humans even existed.

headline: Some Galaxies Are Seen Near the Dawn of Time
body: Powerful telescopes like Hubble and Webb can catch light that has traveled for more than 13 billion years. That light comes from galaxies that formed just a few hundred million years after the Big Bang. So scientists are basically seeing the universe as a baby. These views help them learn how the first stars and galaxies appeared. It's one of science's most amazing feats.
summary: Their light has been traveling for over 13 billion years.

headline: Hubble Has Been Exploring Space Across Four Decades
body: Since it launched in 1990, the Hubble Space Telescope has been one of our most important science tools. It has kept working across four different decades — rare for any spacecraft. In that time it has studied everything from nearby planets to galaxies billions of light-years away. Its long life lets astronomers revisit the same objects and watch how they change.
summary: Launched in 1990, it's still making discoveries today.

headline: Astronauts Repaired a Telescope in Space
body: Unlike most space telescopes, Hubble was built to be fixed by astronauts. Between 1993 and 2009, NASA sent several crews to repair and upgrade it while it orbited Earth. They fixed problems, added new instruments, and kept it running far longer than planned. These were some of the most daring missions in spaceflight history. Thanks to them, Hubble is still going strong.
summary: Crews flew up five times to fix and upgrade it in orbit.

headline: Hubble Helped Discover Dark Energy
body: Hubble helped uncover one of the biggest surprises in science. Astronomers using it found that the universe isn't just expanding — it's speeding up. To explain this, they proposed a mysterious force called dark energy, which seems to make up most of the universe. The discovery won the 2011 Nobel Prize in Physics. Hubble showed the universe behaves in ways no one expected.
summary: It helped reveal a mystery force speeding up the whole universe.

headline: Hubble Lets Scientists Watch the Universe Change
body: Because Hubble has run for decades, scientists can compare photos taken years apart and watch the universe change. It has tracked storms on planets, the growth of supernova wreckage, and the motion of faraway objects. Short missions could never do this. In a way, Hubble lets us watch cosmic history unfold. The universe isn't frozen — and Hubble proves it.
summary: Decades of photos let scientists watch space move in real time.

headline: Hubble Captured Some of History's Most Famous Images
body: Some of the most famous space photos ever taken came from Hubble. Images like the “Pillars of Creation” and the Hubble Deep Field have amazed scientists and the public alike. They're beautiful, but they also carry real science about stars, nebulae, and galaxies. Hubble's photos helped people grasp the scale and wonder of the universe. They've become part of our shared culture.
summary: The 'Pillars of Creation' is a Hubble shot you've probably seen.

headline: Webb Had to Fold Like Origami
body: The James Webb Space Telescope was too big to fit inside its rocket fully open. So engineers designed it to fold up like origami and then unfold itself once in space. The process used hundreds of moving parts and was one of the trickiest things ever attempted. Over several weeks, Webb slowly opened into its full shape. It pulled off a stunning transformation far from Earth.
summary: It was too big for its rocket, so it unfolded itself in space.

headline: Engineers Compared Webb to a Transformer
body: Because of its wild unfolding, some engineers joked that Webb was like a Transformer. After reaching space, it had to open its mirrors, antennas, and a huge sunshield. Each step had to work perfectly before the next could begin — thrilling and nerve-wracking to watch. It took years of planning by thousands of people. Webb is one of the greatest engineering feats in space history.
summary: Mirrors, antennas, a giant shield — all had to unfold perfectly.

headline: Webb's Sunshield Is Like SPF One Million
body: NASA compares Webb's five-layer sunshield to sunscreen with SPF one million. It blocks the Sun's heat so well that the telescope's instruments stay extremely cold. That cold is the whole point — it lets Webb detect faint heat glows from distant galaxies and planets. The hot and cold sides differ by hundreds of degrees. Without the shield, Webb couldn't do its job.
summary: Its five-layer shield blocks the Sun like SPF one million.

headline: Webb Can See More Than 13 Billion Years Into the Past
body: Light takes time to travel, so looking far away means looking far back in time. Webb can catch light that has traveled more than 13 billion years. That light comes from a period shortly after the Big Bang. By studying it, scientists hope to learn how the first stars and galaxies formed. Webb is letting us read the universe's earliest chapters.
summary: It catches light that left soon after the Big Bang.

headline: Webb Launched on Christmas Day
body: The James Webb Space Telescope blasted off on December 25, 2021 — Christmas Day — after years of work and waiting. The launch kicked off one of the boldest science missions ever. Scientists worldwide watched closely, knowing many risky steps still lay ahead in space. Its safe launch was a win for global teamwork and engineering. That Christmas became a historic day for astronomy.
summary: After decades of work, it lifted off on December 25, 2021.

headline: Quasars Are Powered by Feeding Black Holes
body: A quasar is a type of active galaxy with a very bright center. NASA says that extra energy comes from a supermassive black hole feeding on material from the galaxy's dense core. As the material falls inward, it forms a spinning accretion disk. Gravity and friction heat that disk, helping the galaxy's center shine far more brightly than normal.
summary: A galaxy can blaze with extra energy because its central black hole is actively feeding.

headline: Some Galaxy Jets Move Almost at Light Speed
body: Some active galaxies launch narrow beams of energetic particles from their centers. NASA says these jets shoot outward in opposite directions away from the disk around the black hole. They emerge at nearly the speed of light and become powerful sources of radio waves. Quasars, blazars, and radio galaxies are all active galaxies with strong jets that can stretch into huge regions of space between galaxies.
summary: Some black-hole-powered galaxies fire particle jets outward at nearly light speed.

headline: A Quasar Can Depend on Where You're Looking From
body: Quasars are part of a larger family called active galactic nuclei. NASA says the type we see depends on the black hole's mass, how fast it is feeding, whether it has a strong jet, and the angle from which we view it. Some apparent differences come from our viewing direction. With blazars and quasars, we are looking down the jet.
summary: The same kind of active galaxy can look different depending on the viewing angle.

headline: X-Rays Come From Near a Quasar's Black Hole
body: Quasars are studied across many kinds of light, not just what human eyes can see. NASA says X-ray and gamma-ray observations are especially important for high-energy quasars. The X-rays in active galaxies come from very near the black hole. That gives scientists a close look at the physical processes happening in the galaxy's central engine.
summary: Some of a quasar's most useful clues come from X-rays made near its black hole.

headline: A Neutron Star Packs a Sun Into a City
body: A pulsar is a spinning neutron star, and neutron stars are almost unbelievably dense. NASA says they can squeeze the mass of a sun into an object about the size of a city. These stellar leftovers are only about 12.5 miles, or 20 kilometers, across. They form after a massive star runs out of fuel and its core collapses.
summary: A dead star can crush sun-like mass into something about the width of a city.

headline: A Sugar Cube of Neutron Star Stuff Weighs Like a Mountain
body: Neutron star material is so dense that normal comparisons almost break. NASA says one sugar cube of it would weigh about 1 trillion kilograms on Earth. That is about 1 billion tons, or roughly the mass of a mountain. Pulsars are spinning neutron stars, so every pulse comes from one of these city-sized objects packed with extreme matter.
summary: One tiny cube of neutron star material would weigh about as much as a mountain.

headline: Pulsars Flash Like Cosmic Lighthouses
body: Pulsars do not blink because they are turning on and off. NASA says they are spinning neutron stars with strong magnetic fields that send beams of radiation into space. If one of those beams sweeps across Earth, we see a pulse. It works like a lighthouse: the light is always shining, but you only see it when the beam points your way.
summary: A pulsar's flash is a beam sweeping past Earth like a lighthouse in space.

headline: One Magnetar Burst Beat 100,000 Years of Sunlight
body: Magnetars are a special kind of neutron star with extreme magnetic fields. NASA says a normal neutron star's magnetic field is already trillions of times stronger than Earth's, but a magnetar's field is another 1,000 times stronger. One magnetar, called SGR 1806-20, released a huge burst in one-tenth of a second. That burst gave off more energy than the Sun had emitted in the last 100,000 years.
summary: One tiny starquake-like burst released more energy than the Sun had made in 100,000 years.

headline: A White Dwarf Packs a Sun Into Earth's Size
body: A white dwarf is what remains after some stars reach the end of their lives. NASA says a typical white dwarf is about as massive as the Sun, but only slightly bigger than Earth. That makes it one of the densest forms of matter, behind only neutron stars and black holes. It is basically a dead stellar core squeezed into a planet-sized space.
summary: A star's leftover core can hold Sun-like mass in something only a little bigger than Earth.

headline: The Sun Will End as a White Dwarf
body: The Sun will not shine the way it does forever. NASA says that after it becomes a red giant, it will eventually blow off its outer layers. The core will remain intact and become a white dwarf. That white dwarf will sit inside an expanding shell of gas called a planetary nebula before slowly cooling over time.
summary: Our Sun's final form will be a small dead core surrounded by gas it once threw away.

headline: The Universe May Be Too Young for Black Dwarfs
body: White dwarfs slowly cool by sending their heat into space. NASA says that after enough time, they should fade into black lumps of carbon called black dwarfs. But this cooling takes billions of years. Scientists do not think the universe is old enough yet for many, if any, white dwarfs to have become black dwarfs.
summary: Some dead stars may cool into black dwarfs, but the universe may not be old enough to have many yet.

headline: Sirius Wobbled Because of a Hidden Dead Star
body: The first discovered white dwarf was found because Sirius seemed to wobble. NASA says astronomer Friedrich Bessel noticed in 1844 that Sirius had a slight back-and-forth motion, as if it were orbiting something unseen. In 1863, Alvan Clark spotted the mysterious companion. That object was later identified as Sirius B, a white dwarf next to the brighter Sirius A.
summary: Astronomers found a dead star because the brightest star in the night sky was wobbling.

headline: The Sun Will Become a Red Giant
body: The Sun is a main sequence star now, but NASA says it will not stay that way forever. In about 5 billion years, it will become a red giant. That happens after a star like the Sun runs out of hydrogen in its core and its outer layers expand. The result is a much larger star that would appear more orange than red.
summary: The Sun has about 5 billion years before it swells into a giant star.

headline: Red Giants Make Carbon After Hydrogen Runs Out
body: A red giant begins when a star less than eight times the Sun's mass runs low on hydrogen in its core. NASA says the core starts to collapse because fusion is the force fighting gravity. As the core gets squeezed, its temperature and pressure rise. Then helium begins fusing into carbon, releasing energy while hydrogen fusion moves into the star's outer layers.
summary: When hydrogen runs out in the core, a star can start turning helium into carbon.

headline: Red Giants Can Puff Off Their Outer Layers
body: A red giant does not stay calm forever. NASA says it eventually becomes unstable and starts pulsating, periodically expanding and ejecting some of its atmosphere. Over time, all of its outer layers blow away. Those lost layers create an expanding cloud of dust and gas called a planetary nebula, while the remaining core becomes a white dwarf.
summary: A dying star can pulse, shed its atmosphere, and leave a glowing cloud behind.

headline: A Red Giant Is Cooler Because It Gets Bigger
body: When a star grows into a red giant, its outer layers expand far outward. NASA explains that as a star gets bigger, its heat spreads out, making its overall temperature cooler. That is why red giants are cooler than they might sound, even though their cores are heating up. They can look orange-red while deep inside, the star is still changing through intense fusion.
summary: A star can grow enormous and still have a cooler-looking outer surface.

headline: The Oldest Light in the Universe Is Still Around
body: The cosmic microwave background is ancient light from the early universe. NASA says this microwave radiation was released about 375,000 years after the birth of the universe. WMAP mapped it across the full sky to study tiny temperature differences. That light has traveled through space for billions of years, carrying clues from a time long before stars and galaxies existed.
summary: A glow from the baby universe is still traveling through space today.

headline: Tiny Temperature Differences Grew Into Galaxies
body: WMAP studied very small temperature changes in the cosmic microwave background. NASA gives an example where one part of the sky is 2.7251 Kelvin and another is 2.7249 Kelvin. Those differences may sound almost meaningless, but they reveal the early structure of the universe. NASA says the clumped matter from this early pattern eventually went on to form stars and galaxies.
summary: Tiny differences in ancient light helped reveal the seeds of stars and galaxies.

headline: WMAP Measured the Universe's Age
body: WMAP helped turn cosmology into a much more precise science. By mapping tiny fluctuations in the cosmic microwave background, NASA says WMAP revealed the universe's size, matter content, age, geometry, and fate. One of its major results was measuring the universe as 13.77 billion years old. NASA says that number was determined to within half a percent.
summary: A map of ancient microwave light helped pin down the age of the universe.

headline: Ordinary Atoms Are Only About 5% of the Universe
body: WMAP did more than make a map of ancient light. NASA says it helped complete a census of the universe. Its results found that ordinary atoms, also called baryons, make up only about 5% of everything. Dark matter makes up about 25%, while dark energy makes up about 70% and is causing the expansion of the universe to speed up.
summary: The stuff that makes stars, planets, and people is only a small slice of the universe.

headline: Rome Grew From a Small Town Into a Massive Empire
body: Roman tradition says Rome began as a small village along the Tiber River in Italy. Over centuries, it grew through war, trade, and deal-making into one of the mightiest empires ever. At its peak, Rome ruled across Europe, North Africa, and parts of the Middle East. Millions of people lived under the same laws, roads, and rulers. Few civilizations have shaped the world as deeply.
summary: It started as a riverside village and ended up ruling three continents.

headline: Roman Influence Still Lives in Modern Languages
body: Latin, the language of the Roman Empire, never really died — it transformed. Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian all grew out of Latin over many centuries. Even English borrowed thousands of Latin words. So every time someone speaks these languages, a piece of Rome lives on. Languages, it turns out, can outlast the empires that spread them.
summary: Spanish, French, and Italian are basically Latin that kept changing.

headline: Gladiator Battles Came From Etruscan Traditions
body: The gladiator games feel totally Roman, but Rome didn't invent them. Historians think the contests began with the Etruscans, an older civilization in Italy. Rome borrowed the idea and blew it up into huge public shows in arenas like the Colosseum. Gladiator fights became one of Rome's signature forms of entertainment. Their origin shows how often Rome borrowed from its neighbors.
summary: Rome's most famous spectacle was borrowed from an older people.

headline: Rome Lasted for More Than a Thousand Years
body: Rome lasted an astonishingly long time. From its traditional founding in 753 BCE to the fall of the Western Empire in 476 CE, it endured well over a thousand years. In that span, it changed from a kingdom to a republic and then an empire. Its laws and traditions shifted constantly, yet a shared Roman identity held. Few civilizations have stayed powerful for so long.
summary: From founding to fall, it lasted over 1,200 years.

headline: Some Roman Roads Still Survive Today
body: The Romans built a huge network of roads linking cities across their empire. Armies, traders, and travelers could move quickly over long distances. Roman engineers built some roads so well that parts are still standing — and walkable — today, 2,000 years later. The roads helped tie the empire together and boost trade. They're one of the great engineering feats of the ancient world.
summary: Some were built so well you can still walk on them today.

headline: Vikings Reached North America Before Columbus
body: Long before Columbus, Vikings reached North America. At a site called L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, Canada, archaeologists found the remains of a Norse settlement from around the year 1000. That's nearly 500 years before Columbus crossed the Atlantic. The explorers likely came from Greenland and Iceland. Their voyages are some of the earliest known European visits to the Americas.
summary: They crossed the Atlantic around the year 1000 — about 500 years early.

headline: Viking Helmets Didn't Have Horns
body: Despite every movie and cartoon, Vikings didn't wear horned helmets. Real Viking helmets were simple and practical, built for protection in battle. Horns would have been clumsy and dangerous in a fight. The horned look came along much later, from artists and stage shows. It's one of the most stubborn myths about Viking life.
summary: Those iconic horns? Vikings never actually wore them.

headline: Opera Helped Invent the Horned Viking Helmet
body: So where did the horned Viking helmet come from? Mostly the opera stage. In the 1800s, costume designers for operas based on Norse legends gave performers dramatic horned helmets to look striking. The idea spread into paintings, books, and eventually movies, until everyone believed it. But real Viking warriors wore much plainer helmets. A costume choice fooled the whole world.
summary: The horns came from 1800s stage costumes, not real warriors.

headline: Viking Women Could Own Property and Divorce
body: Viking women had more rights than women in many other societies of the time. Historical sources say they could inherit property, ask for divorces, and manage the family's money. While men were away fighting or trading, women often ran the farms and businesses. That gave them real power at home and in their communities. It shows Viking society was more complex than the raider stereotype.
summary: They could own land, divorce, and run the family farm.

headline: Thursday and Friday Come From Norse Gods
body: Two days of the week are named after Norse gods. Thursday comes from Thor, the god of thunder. Friday comes from Frigg, a goddess sometimes linked with Freyja. The names slipped into English through centuries of contact between Norse and Germanic peoples. So every week, people use words that trace straight back to Viking beliefs.
summary: You say Thor's and Frigg's names every single week.

headline: Ancient Greece Wasn't One Country
body: We picture ancient Greece as one nation, but it was really hundreds of separate city-states, each called a polis. Athens, Sparta, Corinth, and others had their own governments, laws, armies, even their own coins. What united them wasn't politics — it was a shared culture: the same language, the same gods, and events like the Olympics. They saw themselves as one people, yet rarely acted as one. Greece was a shared way of life long before it was ever a country.
summary: Hundreds of rival city-states, each with its own laws and army.

headline: Democracy Began in Ancient Athens
body: The word “democracy” comes from Greek and means “rule by the people.” The system took shape in Athens around 508 BCE. Ordinary male citizens gathered in a huge open-air assembly to debate and vote directly on laws and war. Many officials weren't elected at all — they were chosen by lottery, since any citizen was thought fit to serve. It was very different from today's systems, but it planted an idea that still echoes 2,500 years later.
summary: Citizens voted directly on laws — and picked many leaders by lottery.

headline: The Olympic Games Began in Ancient Greece
body: The Olympic Games began at Olympia, where tradition says the first contest was held in 776 BCE to honor Zeus. They were so important that a sacred truce paused wars across Greece so athletes and fans could travel safely. Competitors ran, wrestled, boxed, and raced chariots — and winners got a wreath of olive leaves, not money. The ancient Games lasted nearly 1,200 years. Revived in 1896, they're one of Greece's greatest gifts to the world.
summary: Wars actually paused so athletes could travel safely.

headline: Alexander the Great Reached India
body: Alexander the Great became king of Macedon as a young man, after studying under the philosopher Aristotle. In just over a decade, his armies smashed the Persian Empire and pushed all the way to the Indus River, in modern Pakistan, around 326 BCE. There his exhausted troops refused to go farther, and he turned back. He died at just 32, but his conquests spread Greek ideas across three continents. Historians call the era that followed the Hellenistic Age.
summary: In just over a decade, he conquered all the way to India.

headline: Sparta and Athens Were Completely Different Societies
body: Athens and Sparta were the two giants of ancient Greece — and total opposites. Sparta was a strict military state where boys left home around age seven for brutal training to become soldiers. Athens prized democracy, trade, art, and lively debate, becoming a hub of learning. One worshiped the warrior, the other the thinker. Their long rivalry eventually exploded into the Peloponnesian War, which wore them both down.
summary: One raised soldiers from age seven; the other raised thinkers.

headline: Nobody Knew They Were Living in the Middle Ages
body: People in the medieval centuries had no idea they were living in a “middle” age. The name came much later, from Renaissance scholars who saw themselves reviving ancient Greece and Rome — and dismissed the 1,000 years in between as a gap. To a farmer or knight in the year 1200, their world was just the present. The whole idea of being “in the middle” was added in hindsight. No era thinks of itself as the in-between.
summary: To a knight in 1200, his world was simply 'today.'

headline: The 'Dark Ages' Label Came Later
body: Medieval people never called their time the “Dark Ages.” That gloomy nickname came later, from critics who saw the period as a decline.
